expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of electron affinity trends across the periodic table, exploring the energy change when atoms gain electrons and how this fundamental property varies systematically with atomic structure, effective nuclear charge, and electron configuration to determine chemical reactivity and anion formation.

core_concept.principle: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative (more exothermic) across a period (left to right) due to increasing effective nuclear charge, and becomes less negative (less exothermic) down a group (top to bottom) due to increased atomic radius and electron shielding.
core_concept.explanation: Electron affinity is the energy change that occurs when an electron is added to a gaseous atom to form a negative ion. This property reflects how much an atom 'wants' to gain electrons, which determines its tendency to form anions and its reactivity as a nonmetal. Across a period, increasing nuclear charge with constant shielding makes adding electrons more energetically favorable. Down a group, valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and more shielded, making electron addition less favorable. Electron affinity, along with ionization energy and electronegativity, completes the picture of how atoms interact with electrons in chemical processes.

image.alt: Periodic table showing electron affinity trends with arrows indicating more negative across periods and less negative down groups
image.caption: Electron affinity trends: becomes more negative (exothermic) left to right across periods (→), becomes less negative (less exothermic) top to bottom down groups (↓). Chlorine has most negative EA (-349 kJ/mol); noble gases have positive EA.

sections.introduction: Electron affinity is a fundamental atomic property that quantifies the energy change when an atom gains an electron. Unlike ionization energy (which measures energy to remove electrons), electron affinity measures the energy released or absorbed when electrons are added, providing crucial insight into anion formation and nonmetal reactivity.

key_concept.definition: Electron affinity (EA) is the energy change that occurs when an electron is added to a gaseous atom in its ground state to form a negative ion, measured in kilojoules per mole (kJ/mol), where negative values indicate energy release (exothermic) and positive values indicate energy absorption (endothermic).
key_concept.context: Electron affinity is the opposite process of ionization energy. While IE is always positive (energy must be added to remove electrons), EA can be negative (energy released when electron added) or positive (energy required to force electron onto atom).

classifications.value: Energy change when first electron is added to neutral gaseous atom
characteristics: Can be positive for noble gases and some metals
classifications.behavior: Nonmetals have negative EA₁ (release energy when gaining electrons); metals have less negative or positive EA₁; noble gases have positive EA₁ (resist gaining electrons).

examples.explanation: Cl(g) + e⁻ → Cl⁻(g); EA₁ = -349 kJ/mol. This large negative value explains why chlorine readily forms Cl⁻ ions and is highly reactive as an oxidizing agent.

image.alt: Chlorine atom gaining electron to form chloride ion
image.caption: First electron affinity: energy released when neutral atom gains one electron to form -1 ion.

examples.explanation: Ne(g) + e⁻ → Ne⁻(g); EA₁ = +116 kJ/mol (positive). Energy must be added to force electron onto noble gas, explaining their chemical inertness.

classifications.value: Energy change when second electron is added to -1 gaseous ion
characteristics: Adding electron to negative ion requires energy
classifications.behavior: EA₂ is always positive because adding an electron to an already negative ion requires overcoming electron-electron repulsion. This explains why O²⁻ formation requires energy input despite oxygen's high reactivity.

examples.explanation: O⁻(g) + e⁻ → O²⁻(g); EA₂ = +744 kJ/mol (positive). Despite this energy cost, O²⁻ forms in ionic compounds because lattice energy compensates for the endothermic EA₂.

image.alt: O⁻ ion gaining second electron to form O²⁻
image.caption: Second electron affinity: always endothermic due to electron-electron repulsion in negative ion.

examples.explanation: S⁻(g) + e⁻ → S²⁻(g); EA₂ = +532 kJ/mol. Less positive than oxygen due to larger size reducing electron repulsion.

classifications.value: Energy changes for adding additional electrons to increasingly negative ions
characteristics: Electron repulsion increases with each addition
classifications.behavior: Each successive EA becomes more positive as electron-electron repulsion increases. Third and higher electron affinities are rarely relevant in normal chemistry because the energy costs are prohibitive.

examples.explanation: N can theoretically form N³⁻ (nitride ion), but EA₂ and EA₃ are highly positive. N³⁻ only forms in compounds with very electropositive metals (Li₃N) where lattice energy compensates.

image.alt: Diagram showing successive electron affinities becoming more positive
image.caption: Successive electron affinities: each addition becomes more endothermic due to increasing electron repulsion.

components.definition: Electron affinity equations represent the addition of electrons to gaseous species, with sign convention indicating energy release (negative) or absorption (positive).
components.interpretation: Negative EA = exothermic (energy released, favorable); Positive EA = endothermic (energy required, unfavorable). More negative EA indicates greater tendency to form anions.

worked_examples.title: Writing Electron Affinity Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write the equations representing the first and second electron affinities for oxygen. Identify which is exothermic and which is endothermic, and explain why.

step_by_step.explanation: Write the first electron affinity equation for neutral oxygen.
step_by_step.calculation: EA₁: O(g) + e⁻ → O⁻(g); ΔH = -141 kJ/mol (exothermic)

step_by_step.explanation: Write the second electron affinity equation for O⁻ ion.
step_by_step.calculation: EA₂: O⁻(g) + e⁻ → O²⁻(g); ΔH = +744 kJ/mol (endothermic)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the energy signs and what they mean.
step_by_step.calculation: EA₁: negative = energy released; EA₂: positive = energy required

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why EA₂ is endothermic.
step_by_step.calculation: Adding electron to negative O⁻ ion requires overcoming electron-electron repulsion; energy must be supplied

worked_examples.final_answer: EA₁: O(g) + e⁻ → O⁻(g), ΔH = -141 kJ/mol (exothermic, energy released). EA₂: O⁻(g) + e⁻ → O²⁻(g), ΔH = +744 kJ/mol (endothermic, energy required). EA₁ is exothermic because neutral oxygen attracts the electron; EA₂ is endothermic because the negative O⁻ ion repels the additional electron. Despite the endothermic EA₂, O²⁻ forms in ionic compounds because lattice energy compensates for this energy cost.
worked_examples.concept_applied: First and second electron affinities with sign conventions and energy explanations

sections.section_title: Periodic Trend: Electron Affinity Across a Period
sections.introduction: Moving from left to right across any period of the periodic table, electron affinity generally becomes more negative (more exothermic). This trend reflects the increasing ability of atoms to attract and hold additional electrons as nuclear charge increases.

key_concept.definition: Across a period, electron affinity becomes more negative because protons are added to the nucleus while electrons fill the same principal energy level, increasing effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) without additional shielding.
key_concept.context: This trend explains why nonmetals (right side, more negative EA) readily gain electrons to form anions, while metals (left side, less negative EA) tend to lose electrons instead.

principles.statement: As atomic number increases across a period, the number of protons increases while shielding from inner electrons remains relatively constant, causing Z_eff to increase significantly.
principles.application: Higher Z_eff means the nucleus attracts additional electrons more strongly, making electron addition more energetically favorable (more negative EA).
principles.example: In Period 3: Na (EA = -53 kJ/mol, Z_eff ≈ +1) < Mg (EA ≈ 0) < Cl (EA = -349 kJ/mol, Z_eff ≈ +7). Chlorine releases 6.5× more energy than sodium when gaining an electron.
principles.analogy: Like a stronger magnet: as the magnet (nucleus) gets stronger, it pulls metal filings (electrons) more strongly, releasing more energy when they attach.

principles.statement: As Z_eff increases across a period, atomic radius decreases, bringing the nucleus closer to the added electron where electrostatic attraction is stronger.
principles.application: Smaller radius means added electrons experience stronger attraction, releasing more energy and making EA more negative.
principles.example: In Period 2, atomic radius decreases from Li (152 pm, EA = -60 kJ/mol) to F (71 pm, EA = -328 kJ/mol), correlating with 5.5× more negative EA.
principles.analogy: Like a shorter rope: the shorter the distance (radius), the stronger the pull on the object (electron) from the anchor (nucleus), releasing more energy.

principles.statement: Elements closer to noble gas configuration (filled valence shell) have more negative EA because gaining electrons completes a stable electron configuration.
principles.application: Explains why halogens (one electron from noble gas) have most negative EA in each period; they achieve stable octet by gaining one electron.
principles.example: Fluorine (2s²2p⁵) gains one electron to achieve Ne configuration (2s²2p⁶), releasing -328 kJ/mol. This drive toward noble gas configuration makes halogens highly reactive.

image.alt: Diagram showing halogens gaining electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
image.caption: Halogens have most negative EA because gaining one electron completes their valence shell to achieve noble gas configuration.

components.definition: The change in electron affinity across a period is approximately 20-50 kJ/mol per element for main-group elements, with variations due to electron configuration effects.
components.equation: $\text{EA} \propto -\dfrac{Z_{eff}}{r}$ (more negative with higher Z_eff and smaller r)
components.interpretation: Electron affinity becomes more negative with higher effective nuclear charge and smaller atomic radius, both of which increase across periods.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Electron Affinities in Period 2
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the following Period 2 elements in order of increasingly negative electron affinity: Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F. Explain any exceptions to the general trend.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify that all elements are in the same period (Period 2), so they have the same principal quantum number (n=2) for valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: All elements: valence electrons in n=2 shell

step_by_step.explanation: Note that atomic number increases from left to right: Li (3) → F (9).
step_by_step.calculation: Protons: Li=3, Be=4, B=5, C=6, N=7, O=8, F=9

step_by_step.explanation: Apply the general trend: EA becomes more negative as Z_eff increases across the period.
step_by_step.calculation: Expected order: Li < Be < B < C < N < O < F (increasingly negative)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify exceptions due to electron configuration: Be (2s²) and N (2p³) have less negative EA than expected.
step_by_step.calculation: Actual values: Li (-60) < Be (+240, positive!) < B (-27) < C (-122) < N (+7, nearly zero) < O (-141) < F (-328) kJ/mol

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasingly negative order: Be (positive) < N (nearly zero) < Li < B < C < O < F. Two exceptions occur: (1) Be has positive EA because adding electron to filled 2s² requires entering higher-energy 2p orbital; (2) N has nearly zero EA because adding electron to half-filled 2p³ disrupts stable configuration. These exceptions demonstrate electron configuration effects on electron affinity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Periodic trend in electron affinity with electron configuration exceptions

sections.section_title: Periodic Trend: Electron Affinity Down a Group
sections.introduction: Moving from top to bottom down most groups of the periodic table, electron affinity generally becomes less negative (less exothermic). This trend opposes the period trend and reflects the increasing distance between the nucleus and the added electron.

key_concept.definition: Down a group, electron affinity becomes less negative because each successive element adds a new principal energy level (n increases), placing the added electron farther from the nucleus with increased shielding.
key_concept.context: This trend explains why fluorine is more reactive than chlorine in some contexts, but chlorine actually has more negative EA than fluorine due to electron repulsion effects in fluorine's small size.

principles.statement: Each successive element down a group has valence electrons in a higher principal energy level (n), which are inherently farther from the nucleus and experience weaker attraction.
principles.application: Larger n values mean added electrons are farther from the nucleus, experiencing weaker attraction and releasing less energy (less negative EA).
principles.example: Halogens: F (n=2, EA=-328 kJ/mol) > Cl (n=3, -349) > Br (n=4, -325) > I (n=5, -295) > At (n=6, -270). Note Cl > F exception!
principles.analogy: Like floors in a building: it's harder to grab something from the top floor (high n) than from the ground floor (low n)—the nucleus 'grabs' added electrons less effectively from higher levels.

principles.statement: Inner electron shells shield the added electron from the full nuclear charge, and this shielding increases down a group as more inner shells are added.
principles.application: Increased shielding reduces Z_eff felt by the added electron, making electron addition less energetically favorable despite more protons.
principles.example: Iodine has 46 inner electrons shielding the added electron from 53 protons, resulting in lower effective attraction than fluorine's 2 inner electrons shielding from 9 protons.
principles.analogy: Like layers of clothing: more layers (inner shells) between you and a heat source (nucleus) reduce how much warmth (attraction) you feel at the surface.

principles.statement: As n increases down a group, atomic radius increases significantly, placing the added electron much farther from the nucleus where electrostatic attraction is weaker.
principles.application: Larger radius means weaker electron-nucleus attraction for the added electron, releasing less energy (less negative EA).
principles.example: Fluorine (radius 71 pm, EA=-328 kJ/mol) vs. Iodine (radius 133 pm, EA=-295 kJ/mol): larger iodine has 10% less negative EA due to increased distance.
principles.analogy: Like gravity: a massive planet far away exerts less pull than a smaller planet nearby—distance matters more than mass for added electron attraction.

components.definition: The change in electron affinity down a group is approximately 10-30 kJ/mol per period for main-group elements, with notable exceptions in Group 17 (halogens).
components.interpretation: Electron affinity becomes less negative as atomic radius increases; doubling the radius reduces EA magnitude by approximately 2×.

worked_examples.title: Explaining the Fluorine-Chlorine Electron Affinity Exception
worked_examples.problem: Fluorine (EA = -328 kJ/mol) has a less negative electron affinity than chlorine (EA = -349 kJ/mol), despite fluorine being smaller and having higher effective nuclear charge. Explain this exception.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare atomic sizes of fluorine and chlorine.
step_by_step.calculation: F: 71 pm (very small); Cl: 99 pm (larger)

step_by_step.explanation: Consider electron density in the valence shell.
step_by_step.calculation: F: 7 valence electrons in n=2 (compact); Cl: 7 valence electrons in n=3 (more spread out)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify electron-electron repulsion effect.
step_by_step.calculation: F: high electron density in small 2p sublevel → strong repulsion for added electron; Cl: lower electron density in larger 3p sublevel → less repulsion

step_by_step.explanation: Conclude why Cl has more negative EA despite larger size.
step_by_step.calculation: Cl's larger size reduces electron-electron repulsion enough to overcome the distance effect, making EA more negative than F

worked_examples.final_answer: Chlorine has more negative electron affinity (-349 kJ/mol) than fluorine (-328 kJ/mol) because fluorine's small size (71 pm) creates high electron density in the compact 2p sublevel. When an electron is added to fluorine, strong electron-electron repulsion reduces the energy released. Chlorine's larger 3p sublevel (99 pm) has lower electron density, so the added electron experiences less repulsion. This size-repulsion effect outweighs chlorine's larger distance from the nucleus, making Cl's EA more negative. This is the only group where EA becomes more negative from period 2 to 3.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron-electron repulsion effects on electron affinity in small atoms

worked_examples.title: Comparing Electron Affinities in Group 16 (Chalcogens)
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the Group 16 elements (O, S, Se, Te, Po) in order of increasingly negative electron affinity. Predict which element most readily forms -2 anions and explain why.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify that all elements are in Group 16, so they have the same valence configuration (ns²np⁴).
step_by_step.calculation: All chalcogens: valence = ns²np⁴ (6 valence electrons, need 2 more for octet)

step_by_step.explanation: Note that principal quantum number increases down the group.
Apply the trend: EA becomes less negative as n increases down the group (with O exception).
step_by_step.calculation: Order: O (-141) < S (-200) > Se (-195) > Te (-190) > Po (-180) kJ/mol. Note O < S exception!

step_by_step.explanation: Determine which most readily forms anions.
step_by_step.calculation: S has most negative EA₁ (-200 kJ/mol), but O forms O²⁻ more readily in compounds due to higher lattice energies from smaller size

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasingly negative order (EA₁): Po < Te < Se < O < S. Sulfur has the most negative first electron affinity (-200 kJ/mol) in Group 16, with oxygen being an exception (less negative at -141 kJ/mol) due to electron repulsion in its small 2p sublevel. However, oxygen forms O²⁻ anions more readily in ionic compounds because its smaller size produces higher lattice energies that compensate for the less negative EA. This demonstrates that EA is one factor among many in anion formation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Group trend in electron affinity with size-repulsion exception

sections.introduction: While electron affinity trends are generally consistent, certain exceptions occur due to electron configuration effects, including filled subshell stability, half-filled subshell stability, and electron-electron repulsion in compact orbitals.

key_concept.definition: Exceptions to electron affinity trends occur when electron configuration provides extra stability (filled or half-filled subshells) that resists adding electrons, or when small atomic size creates strong electron-electron repulsion.
key_concept.context: These exceptions are important for understanding why certain elements don't follow simple predictions and reveal the quantum mechanical basis of electron addition energetics.

principles.statement: Elements with filled s-subshells (ns²) have positive or near-zero electron affinities because adding an electron requires entering a higher-energy p orbital.
principles.application: Explains why Be (2s²) has positive EA (+240 kJ/mol) and Mg (3s²) has near-zero EA (-40 kJ/mol), contrary to the general period trend.
principles.example: Beryllium: EA = +240 kJ/mol (endothermic). Adding electron to filled 2s² requires entering 2p orbital, which costs energy. This explains why Be doesn't form Be⁻ ions.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing Be 2s² vs adding electron to 2p
image.caption: Beryllium's filled 2s² subshell resists adding electrons; added electron must enter higher-energy 2p orbital, requiring energy input.

principles.analogy: Like a full parking lot: if all spots in the preferred section (s orbital) are taken, you must park in a farther section (p orbital), which is less desirable and costs more effort.

principles.statement: Elements with half-filled p-subshells (ns²np³) have less negative or near-zero electron affinities because adding an electron disrupts the stable half-filled configuration.
principles.application: Explains why N (2p³) has nearly zero EA (+7 kJ/mol) and P (3p³) has less negative EA (-72 kJ/mol) than expected from the period trend.
principles.example: Nitrogen: EA = +7 kJ/mol (nearly zero). The half-filled 2p³ configuration is stable; adding an electron requires pairing in one orbital, which costs energy through electron repulsion.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing N 2p³ vs adding electron to create pair
image.caption: Nitrogen's half-filled 2p³ subshell is stable; adding electron creates electron pair with repulsion, making EA nearly zero.

principles.analogy: Like a balanced team: three players evenly distributed (half-filled) work well together; adding a fourth player creates crowding (pairing) that reduces efficiency.

principles.statement: Noble gases have positive electron affinities because adding an electron requires entering a new, higher principal energy level, which is highly unfavorable.
principles.application: Explains why noble gases are chemically inert and don't form anions under normal conditions.
principles.example: Neon: EA = +116 kJ/mol; Argon: EA = +96 kJ/mol. Adding electron would require entering (n+1)s orbital, far from nucleus and highly unstable.

image.alt: Orbital diagrams showing noble gas filled shells and next available orbital
image.caption: Noble gases have filled valence shells; added electron must enter next principal level, requiring significant energy input.

principles.analogy: Like a completely full building: to add one more person, you must build a new floor (next energy level), which is very expensive (requires energy).

principles.statement: Period 2 elements (F, O, N) have less negative electron affinities than Period 3 counterparts (Cl, S, P) due to strong electron-electron repulsion in compact 2p orbitals.
principles.application: Explains why Cl > F, S > O, and P ≈ N in electron affinity, reversing the expected group trend for these elements.
principles.example: F (-328 kJ/mol) < Cl (-349 kJ/mol); O (-141 kJ/mol) < S (-200 kJ/mol). Period 3 elements have more negative EA despite larger size because reduced repulsion outweighs distance effect.
principles.analogy: Like a small room vs. large room: adding one more person to a crowded small room (Period 2) creates more discomfort (repulsion) than adding to a spacious large room (Period 3).

worked_examples.title: Explaining Multiple Electron Affinity Exceptions
worked_examples.problem: Compare the electron affinities of N (+7 kJ/mol), O (-141 kJ/mol), and F (-328 kJ/mol) in Period 2. Explain why EA becomes more negative from N to O to F, but why N has nearly zero EA.

step_by_step.explanation: Write the electron configurations for all three elements.
step_by_step.calculation: N: 1s² 2s² 2p³; O: 1s² 2s² 2p⁴; F: 1s² 2s² 2p⁵

step_by_step.explanation: Draw orbital diagrams for the 2p subshells.
step_by_step.calculation: N 2p³: ↑ ↑ ↑ (three unpaired, half-filled); O 2p⁴: ↑↓ ↑ ↑ (one paired); F 2p⁵: ↑↓ ↑↓ ↑ (two paired)

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the stability of each configuration.
step_by_step.calculation: N: half-filled 2p³ is stable (resists adding electron); O: adding electron creates one pair; F: adding electron completes octet (very favorable)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain the EA trend from N to O to F.
step_by_step.calculation: N (+7): half-filled stability resists addition; O (-141): adding electron favorable but some repulsion; F (-328): completing octet very favorable despite repulsion

worked_examples.final_answer: Nitrogen has nearly zero electron affinity (+7 kJ/mol) because its half-filled 2p³ configuration is particularly stable; adding an electron disrupts this stability and creates electron pairing repulsion. Oxygen has more negative EA (-141 kJ/mol) because adding an electron moves toward octet completion, though electron repulsion in the small 2p sublevel limits the energy release. Fluorine has the most negative EA (-328 kJ/mol) because adding one electron completes the octet (achieving Ne configuration), which is highly favorable despite electron repulsion. This demonstrates how electron configuration effects can override simple nuclear charge trends.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron configuration effects on electron affinity: half-filled stability and octet completion

worked_examples.title: Predicting Anion Formation from Electron Affinity
worked_examples.problem: Based on electron affinity values, predict which of the following elements most readily forms -1 anions: C (-122 kJ/mol), N (+7 kJ/mol), O (-141 kJ/mol), F (-328 kJ/mol). Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare the electron affinity values.
Identify which has most negative EA (most exothermic).
step_by_step.calculation: F (-328) has most negative EA → most energy released when gaining electron

step_by_step.explanation: Consider which has positive or near-zero EA (resists gaining electron).
step_by_step.calculation: N (+7) has positive EA → energy required to add electron → won't form N⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Rank tendency to form -1 anions.
step_by_step.calculation: F >> O > C >> N (N essentially doesn't form N⁻)

worked_examples.final_answer: Fluorine most readily forms -1 anions (F⁻) with EA = -328 kJ/mol, releasing the most energy. Oxygen also forms O⁻ readily (EA = -141 kJ/mol). Carbon can form C⁻ but less readily (EA = -122 kJ/mol). Nitrogen essentially does not form N⁻ ions because its EA is positive (+7 kJ/mol), meaning energy must be added to force an electron onto nitrogen. This ranking matches observed chemistry: F⁻ and O²⁻ are common; C⁻ is rare; N⁻ is essentially unknown.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using electron affinity to predict anion formation tendency

sections.introduction: Electron affinity is determined by multiple factors that work together: nuclear charge, atomic radius, electron shielding, and electron configuration. Understanding how these factors interact allows prediction of electron affinity trends and exceptions.

key_concept.definition: Electron affinity depends on four main factors: (1) nuclear charge (Z) - more protons increase attraction for added electron; (2) atomic radius (r) - smaller distance increases attraction; (3) shielding effect (S) - inner electrons reduce effective nuclear charge; (4) electron configuration - filled/half-filled subshells and electron repulsion affect stability.
key_concept.context: These factors combine in the relationship EA ∝ -Z_eff/r, where Z_eff = Z - S. Changes in any factor affect electron affinity, but their relative importance varies across the periodic table, and electron-electron repulsion can override other factors in small atoms.

components.definition: The relative importance of factors varies: across periods, Z_eff dominates; down groups, r and shielding dominate; within a period, electron configuration and repulsion cause exceptions.
components.interpretation: Electron affinity becomes more negative with higher effective nuclear charge and smaller radius, but electron-electron repulsion (especially in small atoms) reduces the magnitude of EA.

principles.statement: Increasing the number of protons in the nucleus increases the electrostatic attraction for an added electron, making EA more negative.
principles.application: Explains general increase in EA magnitude across periods; however, this effect can be offset by increased repulsion or larger radius.
principles.example: Comparing F (9 protons, EA=-328 kJ/mol) to Cl (17 protons, EA=-349 kJ/mol): more protons in Cl would suggest more negative EA, but size and repulsion effects complicate the comparison.
principles.analogy: Like adding weight to a magnet: more weight (protons) makes the magnet (nucleus) pull harder on metal (electrons), releasing more energy when they attach.

principles.statement: Decreasing the distance between the nucleus and the added electron increases electrostatic attraction, making EA more negative.
principles.application: Explains why smaller atoms generally have more negative EA within a period; however, very small atoms experience increased repulsion that can reduce EA.
principles.example: F (71 pm, EA=-328 kJ/mol) vs. I (133 pm, EA=-295 kJ/mol): smaller fluorine has more negative EA due to closer distance, but Cl (99 pm, -349 kJ/mol) exceeds F due to repulsion effects.

image.alt: Graph showing relationship between atomic radius and electron affinity
image.caption: Electron affinity generally becomes more negative with smaller atomic radius, but exceptions occur due to electron repulsion in very small atoms.

principles.analogy: Like a shorter rope: the shorter the distance (radius), the stronger the pull on the object (electron) from the anchor (nucleus), releasing more energy.

principles.statement: Inner electrons shield the added electron from the full nuclear charge, reducing effective nuclear charge and making EA less negative.
principles.application: Explains why EA becomes less negative down groups despite increasing nuclear charge; inner shells provide increasing shielding.
principles.example: F (2 inner electrons, Z_eff high, EA=-328) vs. Cl (10 inner electrons, Z_eff lower relative to Z, EA=-349): shielding increases but Cl's larger size reduces repulsion, giving more negative EA.
principles.analogy: Like a crowd blocking a view: more people between you and the stage (inner electrons between added electron and nucleus) reduce how well you can see (feel attraction).

principles.statement: Electron-electron repulsion in compact orbitals reduces the energy released when adding an electron, making EA less negative than expected from nuclear charge and radius alone.
principles.application: Explains Period 2 anomalies (F < Cl, O < S, N ≈ 0) where small atomic size creates high electron density and strong repulsion.
principles.example: F (small, high electron density, EA=-328) vs. Cl (larger, lower electron density, EA=-349): Cl's larger size reduces repulsion enough to overcome distance effect, giving more negative EA.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron density and repulsion in small vs. large atoms
image.caption: Electron-electron repulsion: small atoms (F) have high electron density causing strong repulsion; larger atoms (Cl) have lower density and less repulsion.

principles.analogy: Like a crowded elevator: adding one more person to a packed small elevator (small atom) creates more discomfort (repulsion) than adding to a spacious large elevator (larger atom).

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Multiple Factors: O vs. S Electron Affinity
worked_examples.problem: Oxygen (Z=8) has a less negative electron affinity (-141 kJ/mol) than sulfur (Z=16, -200 kJ/mol), despite oxygen being smaller and having electrons closer to the nucleus. Analyze all factors to explain this exception.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare nuclear charge: S has more protons.
step_by_step.calculation: O: Z=8; S: Z=16 → S should have more negative EA based on nuclear charge alone

step_by_step.explanation: Compare atomic radius: O is smaller.
step_by_step.calculation: O: 66 pm; S: 104 pm → O should have more negative EA based on radius alone

step_by_step.explanation: Compare shielding: S has more inner electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: O: 2 inner (1s²); S: 10 inner ([Ne] core) → S has more shielding, should reduce EA

step_by_step.explanation: Compare electron-electron repulsion: this is the deciding factor.
step_by_step.calculation: O: 6 valence electrons in compact 2p (high density, strong repulsion); S: 6 valence electrons in larger 3p (lower density, less repulsion) → S has less repulsion, more negative EA

worked_examples.final_answer: Despite oxygen being smaller (66 pm vs. 104 pm) which should favor more negative EA, sulfur has more negative electron affinity (-200 vs. -141 kJ/mol) because oxygen's compact 2p sublevel has high electron density, creating strong electron-electron repulsion when an electron is added. Sulfur's larger 3p sublevel has lower electron density, so the added electron experiences less repulsion. The repulsion effect in oxygen outweighs the radius advantage, making sulfur's EA more negative. This demonstrates how electron-electron repulsion can override radius and nuclear charge effects.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple factor analysis showing electron repulsion can override nuclear charge and radius effects

sections.introduction: Understanding electron affinity trends enables chemists to predict anion formation, oxidizing agent strength, compound stability, and material properties. These applications demonstrate the practical importance of electron affinity in chemistry and technology.

key_concept.definition: Electron affinity trends are applied in predicting nonmetal reactivity, identifying oxidizing agents, designing ionic compounds, understanding battery chemistry, and explaining biological electron transfer processes.
key_concept.context: From selecting oxidizing agents for industrial processes to understanding why certain elements form specific anions, electron affinity is a fundamental tool for chemical prediction and material design.

principles.statement: Elements with more negative electron affinities are stronger oxidizing agents because they release more energy when gaining electrons from other substances.
principles.application: Explains why halogens (especially F, Cl) are powerful oxidizing agents; why noble gases (positive EA) are not oxidizing agents.
principles.example: Fluorine (EA=-328 kJ/mol) is the strongest oxidizing agent, able to oxidize water, glass, and most metals. Iodine (EA=-295 kJ/mol) is a weaker oxidizing agent, used in gentler oxidation reactions.

image.caption: Oxidizing strength correlates with electron affinity: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂ matches EA trend (with F exception).

principles.analogy: Like a vacuum cleaner: stronger suction (more negative EA) picks up debris (electrons) more aggressively from other surfaces.

principles.statement: Elements with more negative electron affinities form more stable anions; elements with positive EA don't form stable anions under normal conditions.
principles.application: Predicts which anions are common (F⁻, Cl⁻, O²⁻, S²⁻) vs. rare or unknown (N⁻, noble gas anions).
principles.example: Cl⁻ is very stable (EA=-349 kJ/mol) and common in nature; N⁻ is essentially unknown (EA=+7 kJ/mol) because forming it requires energy input.
principles.analogy: Like a comfortable seat: some seats naturally fit certain people (stable anions); forcing someone into the wrong seat (unstable anion) requires effort and doesn't last.

principles.statement: The combination of metal ionization energy and nonmetal electron affinity determines whether ionic compound formation is energetically favorable.
principles.application: Explains why NaCl forms readily (Na low IE, Cl very negative EA) but NaNe doesn't form (Ne positive EA).
principles.example: NaCl formation: Na IE₁=+496 kJ/mol (cost), Cl EA=-349 kJ/mol (release), lattice energy=-787 kJ/mol (release). Net: exothermic, favorable. NaNe: Ne EA=+116 kJ/mol (cost), no lattice energy compensation → doesn't form.

image.alt: Energy diagram showing ionic compound formation energetics
image.caption: Ionic compound formation: metal IE (cost) + nonmetal EA (release) + lattice energy (release) = net energy change.

principles.statement: Electron affinity affects electrode potentials in batteries; materials with very negative EA make good cathode materials (electron acceptors).
principles.application: Guides selection of cathode materials for batteries; explains why fluorine compounds are being researched for high-energy batteries.
principles.example: Li-ion batteries use transition metal oxides as cathodes; fluorine-doped cathodes show higher voltages due to F's very negative EA enhancing electron acceptance.
principles.analogy: Like a water wheel: higher drop (more negative EA) generates more power (voltage) when electrons 'fall' to the cathode.

principles.statement: Electron affinity influences electron transfer in biological systems; molecules with appropriate EA values serve as electron carriers in respiration and photosynthesis.
principles.application: Explains why oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor in aerobic respiration (very negative EA); why certain cofactors (NAD⁺, FAD) accept electrons at specific points.
principles.example: Oxygen (EA=-141 kJ/mol for O→O⁻, plus lattice/hydration effects) is the final electron acceptor in cellular respiration, releasing energy that drives ATP synthesis.

image.alt: Electron transport chain showing electron affinity gradient
image.caption: Biological electron transfer: electrons flow from low EA molecules to high EA molecules (oxygen), releasing energy for ATP production.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Oxidizing Agent Strength
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following halogens in order of oxidizing strength: F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂. Use electron affinity data: F (-328 kJ/mol), Cl (-349 kJ/mol), Br (-325 kJ/mol), I (-295 kJ/mol). Explain any discrepancy between EA and observed oxidizing strength.

step_by_step.explanation: List the electron affinity values for each halogen.
Rank by EA (more negative = stronger oxidizing agent based on EA alone).
step_by_step.calculation: By EA: Cl > F > Br > I

step_by_step.explanation: Consider additional factors affecting oxidizing strength.
step_by_step.calculation: Oxidizing strength also depends on: bond dissociation energy (F-F weak), hydration energy (F⁻ highly hydrated), overall reaction energetics

step_by_step.explanation: Determine actual oxidizing strength order.
step_by_step.calculation: Actual oxidizing strength: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂. F₂ is strongest despite Cl having more negative EA because F-F bond is weak and F⁻ hydration is very exothermic

worked_examples.final_answer: By electron affinity alone: Cl > F > Br > I. However, actual oxidizing strength: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂. Fluorine is the strongest oxidizing agent despite having less negative EA than chlorine because: (1) F-F bond dissociation energy is low (easy to break), (2) F⁻ hydration energy is very exothermic (stabilizes product), (3) overall reaction energetics favor F₂ reduction. This demonstrates that EA is one factor among many in determining oxidizing strength, though it correlates well within most groups.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron affinity in oxidizing agent prediction with consideration of multiple factors

worked_examples.title: Explaining Why NaCl Forms but NaNe Does Not
worked_examples.problem: Both chlorine and neon are in Period 3. Sodium forms NaCl readily but does not form NaNe. Explain using electron affinity and other energetic factors.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare electron affinities of Cl and Ne.
step_by_step.calculation: Cl: EA = -349 kJ/mol (releases energy); Ne: EA = +116 kJ/mol (requires energy)

step_by_step.explanation: Consider the energy balance for NaCl formation.
step_by_step.calculation: Na IE₁ = +496 kJ/mol (cost); Cl EA = -349 kJ/mol (release); Lattice energy = -787 kJ/mol (release). Net = -640 kJ/mol (exothermic, favorable)

step_by_step.explanation: Consider the energy balance for hypothetical NaNe formation.
step_by_step.calculation: Na IE₁ = +496 kJ/mol (cost); Ne EA = +116 kJ/mol (cost); No lattice energy (Ne⁻ unstable). Net = +612 kJ/mol (endothermic, unfavorable)

step_by_step.explanation: Conclude why NaCl forms but NaNe does not.
step_by_step.calculation: NaCl: net exothermic → forms spontaneously; NaNe: net endothermic → doesn't form

worked_examples.final_answer: NaCl forms readily because the overall process is exothermic (-640 kJ/mol): sodium's ionization energy cost (+496) is more than compensated by chlorine's electron affinity release (-349) and large lattice energy (-787). NaNe does not form because neon has positive electron affinity (+116 kJ/mol), meaning energy must be added to form Ne⁻. Combined with sodium's ionization cost and no lattice energy compensation (Ne⁻ is unstable), the net process is highly endothermic (+612 kJ/mol). This demonstrates how electron affinity determines whether ionic compounds can form.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron affinity in ionic compound formation energetics

sections.section_title: Relationship Between Electron Affinity and Other Properties
sections.introduction: Electron affinity doesn't exist in isolation—it correlates strongly with other periodic properties including ionization energy, electronegativity, atomic radius, and nonmetallic character. Understanding these relationships provides a unified view of periodic trends.

key_concept.definition: Electron affinity is directly related to ionization energy and electronegativity (atoms that hold electrons tightly have high IE, high EN, and negative EA), and inversely related to atomic radius and metallic character (smaller, less metallic atoms have more negative EA).
key_concept.context: These correlations allow prediction of multiple properties from electron affinity data and explain why certain elements exhibit characteristic chemical behaviors across multiple dimensions.

principles.statement: Electron affinity and ionization energy are generally correlated: elements with high IE tend to have more negative EA because both reflect strong electron-nucleus attraction.
principles.application: Explains why halogens have both high IE and very negative EA (hold their electrons tightly and attract additional electrons); why alkali metals have low IE and less negative EA.
principles.example: Fluorine (IE=1681 kJ/mol, EA=-328 kJ/mol) vs. Sodium (IE=496 kJ/mol, EA=-53 kJ/mol): fluorine holds electrons tightly in both losing and gaining contexts.
principles.analogy: Like a strong grip: someone who holds their own belongings tightly (high IE) also grabs new items strongly (negative EA).

image.alt: Graph showing relationship between electron affinity and ionization energy
image.caption: Electron affinity and ionization energy show general direct correlation: elements with high IE also have more negative EA.

principles.statement: Electron affinity is directly proportional to electronegativity: atoms that attract bonding electrons strongly (high EN) also attract free electrons strongly (more negative EA).
principles.application: Explains why fluorine has both highest EN and very negative EA; why cesium has both lowest EN and least negative EA.
principles.example: Fluorine (EN=3.98, EA=-328 kJ/mol) vs. Cesium (EN=0.79, EA=-46 kJ/mol): both properties track together across periodic table.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet: a strong magnet (high EN) both holds shared metal tightly (bonding) and attracts new metal strongly (EA).

image.alt: Graph showing direct relationship between electron affinity and electronegativity
image.caption: Electron affinity and electronegativity show strong direct correlation: elements with high EN also have more negative EA.

principles.statement: Electron affinity is inversely proportional to atomic radius: smaller atoms have more negative EA because added electrons are closer to the nucleus and experience stronger attraction.
principles.application: Explains general trend across periods (smaller atoms → more negative EA); exceptions occur when small size creates high electron repulsion.
principles.example: Period 3: Na (186 pm, EA=-53) < Cl (99 pm, EA=-349). Smaller chlorine has 6.5× more negative EA due to closer distance.
principles.analogy: Like a shorter rope: the shorter the distance (radius), the stronger the pull on the object (added electron) from the anchor (nucleus).

image.alt: Graph showing inverse relationship between electron affinity and atomic radius
image.caption: Electron affinity and atomic radius show inverse correlation: smaller atoms generally have more negative EA, with Period 2 exceptions.

principles.statement: Electron affinity is directly related to nonmetallic character: more negative EA indicates higher nonmetallic character (tendency to gain electrons); less negative EA indicates metallic character.
principles.application: Explains why nonmetals (right side, very negative EA) form anions; metals (left side, less negative EA) form cations.
principles.example: Chlorine (EA=-349 kJ/mol, nonmetallic) forms Cl⁻; Sodium (EA=-53 kJ/mol, metallic) forms Na⁺; the EA difference reflects their opposite chemical behaviors.
principles.analogy: Like shopping behavior: someone who eagerly acquires items (negative EA, nonmetallic) vs. someone who readily gives items away (metallic, low IE).

image.alt: Periodic table showing relationship between electron affinity and nonmetallic character
image.caption: More negative electron affinity correlates with higher nonmetallic character; less negative EA correlates with metallic character.

components.definition: The relationships between electron affinity and other properties can be expressed as correlations that enable multi-property prediction.
components.interpretation: Electron affinity serves as a central property that correlates with multiple other periodic trends, making it a key indicator of overall chemical behavior, especially for nonmetals.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Multiple Properties from Electron Affinity
worked_examples.problem: Element X has EA = -328 kJ/mol; Element Y has EA = -53 kJ/mol. Predict which has higher ionization energy, smaller atomic radius, higher electronegativity, and more nonmetallic character.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare electron affinities to determine relative positions.
step_by_step.calculation: X (-328) << Y (-53) → X has much more negative EA, likely halogen; Y has less negative EA, likely alkali metal

step_by_step.explanation: Apply EA-IE relationship (direct correlation).
step_by_step.calculation: More negative EA → higher IE → X has higher ionization energy

step_by_step.explanation: Apply EA-radius relationship (inverse correlation).
step_by_step.calculation: More negative EA → smaller radius → X has smaller atomic radius

step_by_step.explanation: Apply EA-EN relationship (direct correlation).
step_by_step.calculation: More negative EA → higher EN → X has higher electronegativity

step_by_step.explanation: Apply EA-nonmetallic character relationship (direct correlation).
step_by_step.calculation: More negative EA → more nonmetallic → X has more nonmetallic character

worked_examples.final_answer: Element X (EA = -328 kJ/mol, likely fluorine): higher ionization energy, smaller atomic radius, higher electronegativity, more nonmetallic character. Element Y (EA = -53 kJ/mol, likely sodium): lower ionization energy, larger atomic radius, lower electronegativity, more metallic character. The 6× EA difference indicates X and Y are on opposite sides of the periodic table with correspondingly opposite properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using electron affinity to predict multiple correlated periodic properties

summary: Electron affinity (EA) is the energy change when an electron is added to a gaseous atom, measured in kJ/mol. Negative EA = exothermic (energy released, favorable); positive EA = endothermic (energy required, unfavorable). EA becomes more negative across periods (left to right) due to increasing effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) with constant shielding, making electron addition more favorable. EA becomes less negative down groups (top to bottom) due to increased principal quantum number (n), larger atomic radius, and increased shielding, placing added electrons farther from nucleus. Exceptions occur: (1) Group 2 (filled s²) has positive/near-zero EA (Be=+240); (2) Group 15 (half-filled p³) has near-zero EA (N=+7); (3) Period 2 elements have less negative EA than Period 3 counterparts (F=-328 < Cl=-349, O=-141 < S=-200) due to electron-electron repulsion in compact orbitals. EA directly correlates with ionization energy, electronegativity, and nonmetallic character; inversely correlates with atomic radius. Applications include predicting oxidizing agent strength (more negative EA = stronger oxidizer), anion stability (very negative EA = stable anions), ionic compound formation (EA + IE + lattice energy determine favorability), battery chemistry (cathode materials), and biological electron transfer (oxygen as terminal acceptor). Chlorine has most negative EA (-349 kJ/mol); noble gases have positive EA. EA ∝ -Z_eff/r summarizes key factors: nuclear charge, shielding, radius, and electron repulsion determine how favorably atoms gain electrons.
